title: Walks

tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and the edge classifications of def def-edge-relations. Let $v, w \in J \cup V$.

  1. Walk. A walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is, for some integer $n \ge 0$, a tuple \[ (v_0, a_0, v_1, a_1, \dots, v_{n-1}, a_{n-1}, v_n) \] consisting of $n + 1$ vertices $v_0, v_1, \dots, v_n \in J \cup V$ and $n$ ordered pairs $a_0, a_1, \dots, a_{n-1} \in (J \cup V) \times (J \cup V)$, such that:
    1. $v_0 = v$ and $v_n = w$;
    2. for every $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}$, \[ \bigl(a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \;\land\; a_k \in E \cup L\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_k = (v_{k+1}, v_k) \;\land\; a_k \in E\bigr). \]

    The case $n = 0$ is admitted: the trivial walk is the single-vertex tuple $(v_0)$ with $v_0 = v = w$, no edges, and condition (b) vacuously satisfied; it is a walk only when $v = w$. For $n \ge 1$, vertices in the tuple may repeat, i.e.\ the same node may appear multiple times among $v_0, \dots, v_n$.

    End-node classifications. For a non-trivial walk ($n \ge 1$) we further classify behaviour at the two end-nodes:

    • the walk is into $v_0$ iff $a_0$ is an edge into $v_0$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.; concretely, iff $\bigl(a_0 = (v_1, v_0) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L\bigr)$;
    • the walk is out of $v_0$ iff $a_0$ is an edge out of $v_0$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item iii.; concretely, iff $a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in E$;
    • the walk is into $v_n$ iff $a_{n-1}$ is an edge into $v_n$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.; concretely, iff $\bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in L\bigr)$;
    • the walk is out of $v_n$ iff $a_{n-1}$ is an edge out of $v_n$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item iii.; concretely, iff $a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E$.

    On the trivial walk ($n = 0$) all four classifications are vacuously false (neither $a_0$ nor $a_{n-1}$ exists). On a non-trivial walk with a bidirected first edge ($a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L$) the walk is into $v_0$ but not out of $v_0$; hence “into $v_0$” and “out of $v_0$” are mutually exclusive but not jointly exhaustive over walks (and analogously at $v_n$).

  2. Directed walk. A directed walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ (for some $n \ge 0$) that, in addition, satisfies \[ a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in E \qquad \text{for every } k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\}. \] The trivial walk ($n = 0$, $v = w$) is admitted as a directed walk from $v$ to itself.
  3. Bidirected walk. A bidirected walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ (for some $n \ge 0$) that, in addition, satisfies \[ a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L \qquad \text{for every } k \in \{0, 1, \dots, n - 1\} \] (equivalently, by the symmetry of $L$ from def def-cdmg, $(v_{k+1}, v_k) \in L$ for every such $k$). The trivial walk ($n = 0$, $v = w$) is admitted as a bidirected walk from $v$ to itself.
  4. Collider walk. A collider walk from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ (for some $n \ge 0$) satisfying the case-distinguished constraints below.

    • Case $n = 0$: no further constraint; the trivial walk is a collider walk from $v$ to itself when $v = w$.
    • Case $n = 1$: the single edge $a_0$ is required to be bidirected, namely \[ a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L \qquad \text{(equivalently, } (v, w) \in L\text{).} \] Purely directed edges $(v, w) \in E$ or $(w, v) \in E$ are not admitted as collider walks of length $1$.
    • Case $n \ge 2$: the edges satisfy: \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
    • (the first edge places an arrowhead at $v_1$) $\bigl(a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L\bigr)$;
    • (every interior edge is bidirected) for every $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, n - 2\}$, $a_k = (v_k, v_{k+1}) \in L$;
    • (the last edge places an arrowhead at $v_{n-1}$) $\bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E\bigr) \,\lor\, \bigl(a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in L\bigr)$. \end{enumerate} Under (a)–(c), every interior node $v_k$ (i.e.\ $1 \le k \le n - 1$) has an arrowhead pointing toward it from each of its two incident walk-edges $a_{k-1}$ and $a_k$, recovering the verbal characterisation “every node strictly between $v$ and $w$ is a collider”.

    Reconciliation with the source block's $n = 1$ inline note. The source block contains the inline remark “Note that for $n = 1$ this reads: $v \sus w \in G$”. This remark is overridden by the strict reading of Case $n = 1$ above: for $n = 1$, a collider walk requires its unique edge to be bidirected, $a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L$. The relaxed reading “any $v \sus w$ edge” is not adopted here; in particular, neither $(v, w) \in E$ nor $(w, v) \in E$ qualifies as a collider walk of length $1$.

  5. Path. A walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ in $G$ is called a path iff its vertex sequence contains no repetitions, i.e.\ $v_i \ne v_j$ whenever $0 \le i < j \le n$. (Equivalently, the tuple $(v_0, v_1, \dots, v_n)$ consists of $n + 1$ pairwise distinct entries. The trivial walk $n = 0$ is vacuously a path.)
  6. Bifurcation. A bifurcation between $v$ and $w$ in $G$ is a walk $(v_0, a_0, v_1, \dots, a_{n-1}, v_n)$ from $v$ to $w$ for which there exists an index $k$ with $1 \le k \le n$ (in particular $n \ge 1$) such that all of the following hold:

    1. Distinct end-nodes appearing exactly once. $v \ne w$, and the end-nodes each occur exactly once in the vertex sequence: \[ v_0 \notin \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\} \qquad\text{and}\qquad v_n \notin \{v_0, v_1, \dots, v_{n-1}\}. \]
    2. Left subwalk is a directed walk from $v_{k-1}$ to $v_0$. For every $j \in \{0, 1, \dots, k - 2\}$, \[ a_j = (v_{j+1}, v_j) \in E. \] (When $k = 1$ the left subwalk is the trivial walk $(v_0)$ and this constraint is vacuous.)
    3. Right subwalk is a directed walk from $v_k$ to $v_n$. For every $j \in \{k, k + 1, \dots, n - 1\}$, \[ a_j = (v_j, v_{j+1}) \in E. \] (When $k = n$ the right subwalk is the trivial walk $(v_n)$ and this constraint is vacuous.)
    4. Middle (hinge) edge. The edge $a_{k-1}$ between $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ satisfies \[ \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L\bigr). \]
    5. (Addition: each end-node has exactly one arrowhead pointing toward it.) $a_0$ is an edge into $v_0$ and $a_{n-1}$ is an edge into $v_n$, in the sense of def def-edge-relations, item ii.; equivalently, both end-nodes $v_0$ and $v_n$ have exactly one arrowhead pointing toward them (contributed by their unique incident walk-edges $a_0$ and $a_{n-1}$ respectively, uniqueness following from clause (a)).

      Spelled out as a case analysis on $k$:

      • if $k \ge 2$: clause (b) gives $a_0 = (v_1, v_0) \in E$, which is automatically an edge into $v_0$ (no further restriction);
      • if $k = 1$: $a_0 = a_{k-1}$ is the middle edge; both alternatives admitted by (d) — namely $a_0 = (v_1, v_0) \in E$ and $a_0 = (v_0, v_1) \in L$ — are edges into $v_0$, so no further restriction at $v_0$;
      • if $k \le n - 1$: clause (c) gives $a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in E$, which is automatically an edge into $v_n$ (no further restriction);
      • if $k = n$: $a_{n-1} = a_{k-1}$ is the middle edge; among the two alternatives admitted by (d), only the bidirected alternative $a_{n-1} = (v_{n-1}, v_n) \in L$ is an edge into $v_n$, whereas the directed alternative $a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E$ places its arrowhead at $v_{n-1}$ rather than $v_n$ and is therefore excluded.

      In particular, the degenerate configuration “$k = n$ together with the directed hinge $a_{n-1} = (v_n, v_{n-1}) \in E$” — which would otherwise admit a tuple of the form $v_n \to v_{n-1} \to \cdots \to v_1 \to v_0$ (a plain directed walk from $v_n$ to $v_0$) as a “bifurcation” — is not a bifurcation under the present definition.

    Source of the bifurcation. If clause (d) is realised by the directed alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_k, v_{k-1}) \in E$, then we call $v_k$ the source of the bifurcation. If clause (d) is realised by the bidirected alternative $a_{k-1} = (v_{k-1}, v_k) \in L$, no source is defined. Combined with clause (e), whenever a source $v_k$ is defined we automatically have $1 \le k \le n - 1$ (the case $k = n$ with directed hinge is excluded by (e)), so the source $v_k$ is an interior vertex of the walk and is distinct from both end-nodes $v_0 = v$ and $v_n = w$.

lean_blocks.code:
def WalkStep (G : CDMG Node) (u : Node) (a : Node × Node) (v : Node) : Prop :=
  (a = (u, v) ∧ (a ∈ G.E ∨ a ∈ G.L)) ∨ (a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E)

def vertices : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → List Node
  | _, _, .nil v _ => [v]
  | u, _, .cons _ _ _ p => u :: p.vertices

def IsColliderRest : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (.nil _ _) =>
      (a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E) ∨ (a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L)
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)) =>
      a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L ∧ p.IsColliderRest

def IsBifurcationWithSplit : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (.nil _ _), 0 => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)), 0 =>
      ((a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E) ∨ (a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L)) ∧ p.IsDirectedWalk
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p, k + 1 =>
      a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsBifurcationWithSplit k

def IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → ℕ → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _, _ => False
  | _, _, .cons _ _ _ (.nil _ _), 0 => False
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)), 0 =>
      a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsDirectedWalk
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p, k + 1 =>
      a = (v, u) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit k

inductive Walk (G : CDMG Node) : Node → Node → Type _ where
  | nil (v : Node) (hv : v ∈ G) : Walk G v v
  | cons {u w : Node} (v : Node) (a : Node × Node)
      (h : G.WalkStep u a v) (p : Walk G v w) : Walk G u w

def intoStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | u, _, .cons _ a _ _ => G.into u a

def outOfStart : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => False
  | u, _, .cons _ a _ _ => G.outOf u a

def intoEnd {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  match p.edges.getLast? with
  | none => False
  | some a => G.into v a

def outOfEnd {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  match p.edges.getLast? with
  | none => False
  | some a => G.outOf v a

def IsDirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.E ∧ p.IsDirectedWalk

def IsBidirectedWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ p => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L ∧ p.IsBidirectedWalk

def IsColliderWalk : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → Prop
  | _, _, .nil _ _ => True
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (.nil _ _) => a = (u, v) ∧ a ∈ G.L
  | u, _, .cons v a _ (p@(.cons _ _ _ _)) =>
      (a = (u, v) ∧ (a ∈ G.E ∨ a ∈ G.L)) ∧ p.IsColliderRest
def IsPath {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop := p.vertices.Nodup

def IsBifurcation {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) : Prop :=
  u ≠ v ∧
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationWithSplit i

def IsBifurcationSource {u v : Node} (p : Walk G u v) (x : Node) : Prop :=
  u ≠ v ∧
  u ∉ p.vertices.tail ∧
  v ∉ p.vertices.dropLast ∧
  ∃ i, p.IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit i ∧ p.vertices[i + 1]? = some x

addition_to_the_LN:
[bifurcation_right_chain_trivial_is_just_directed_walk] As an additional constraint on the bifurcation form, both endnodes $v_0$ and $v_n$ must have exactly one arrowhead pointing towards them. In particular, this excludes the degenerate case $k = n$ with directed hinge $v_{n-1} \hut v_n$, in which the walk reduces to a directed walk $v_0 \leftarrow v_1 \leftarrow \cdots \leftarrow v_n$ and $v_n$ has no arrowhead pointing towards it.

[collider_walk_n1_form_contradicts_inline_note] For $n=1$, a collider walk from $v$ to $w$ requires a bidirected edge $v \huh w \in E$ (arrowheads at both endpoints), as dictated by the form $v_0 \suh v_1$ combined with $v_{n-1} \hus v_n$ collapsing onto the single edge. The inline note "$v \sus w \in G$" is to be read in this stricter sense and does *not* admit purely directed edges $v \tuh w$ or $v \hut w$ as collider walks of length 1.
lean_explanation:
This row introduces the central data type of the chapter — `Walk` — together with eight helper definitions and a family of derived predicates. A few Lean-level idioms recur, so it helps to read them once up front rather than at every block.

The two `variable` blocks are not declarations in their own right; they are scoping directives that auto-bind implicit parameters into every subsequent declaration in the surrounding namespace. The first, `variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]`, fixes a polymorphic vertex carrier `Node` together with decidable equality on it (needed so we can talk about list membership, `Nodup`, etc.). The second, `variable {G : CDMG Node}`, fixes a graph `G` once and for all inside the `Walk` namespace so that signatures like `def vertices : … Walk G u v → List Node` need not repeat `(G : CDMG Node)` each time. Because both binders are curly-braced they remain implicit at call sites, where `G` is inferred from the walk's type.

`inductive Walk` declares `Walk G u v` as a new datatype indexed by its endpoints, with two constructors: `nil v hv` for the trivial single-vertex walk (forcing `u = v` at the type level) and `cons v a h p` to glue one edge in front of an existing walk. This is the same shape mathlib uses for `SimpleGraph.Walk`, and it lets every consumer destructure a walk by pattern-matching on `nil` versus `cons`.

The recursive `def`s such as `def vertices : ∀ {u v : Node}, Walk G u v → List Node | _, _, .nil v _ => [v] | u, _, .cons _ _ _ p => u :: p.vertices` use Lean's equation-compiler syntax. Each line after `|` matches the explicit and implicit arguments in order; underscores discard arguments not needed on that branch, and the leading dot in `.nil` / `.cons` tells Lean to look up the constructor inside the expected type rather than the ambient namespace. The recursive call `p.vertices` is dot-notation for `Walk.vertices p`. Several predicates additionally take a `ℕ` counter as the last argument and pattern-match on `0` versus `k + 1`, which is the standard idiom for "walk through the structure while counting down".

`WalkStep` is a single-edge constraint factored out so that `Walk.cons` can carry it as one clean hypothesis `h` rather than re-spelling the disjunction at every step. The four end-node classifiers `intoStart`, `outOfStart`, `intoEnd`, `outOfEnd` are independent predicates rather than a four-way enum because the trivial walk is none of them and a bidirected first edge is `intoStart` but not `outOfStart` — they are mutually exclusive yet not jointly exhaustive. `IsColliderRest` and `IsBifurcationWithSplit` are auxiliary recursions: the former carries the "interior + last edge" half of an `n ≥ 2` collider walk after `IsColliderWalk` has peeled off the first edge; the latter is indexed by a natural number tracking how many reverse-directed left-arm edges remain before the hinge (so `IsBifurcation` simply existentially quantifies `∃ i, p.IsBifurcationWithSplit i`). `IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit` is the variant in which the hinge edge is specifically a directed edge `(v_k, v_{k-1}) ∈ E`, used by `IsBifurcationSource` to locate the `v_k` whose two outgoing directed sub-walks reach the two endpoints.

All derived notions live in `Prop` rather than `Bool`: they are logical hypotheses, not Boolean flags, so a pair `(p : Walk G u v) (hp : p.IsDirectedWalk)` is the idiomatic way to carry "a directed walk" without introducing a separate inductive type. `List`, `List.Nodup`, `List.getLast?`, `List.tail`, and `List.dropLast` are the standard mathlib list operations; `getLast?` returns `Option` (matching on `none` is how the trivial walk is detected when reaching for `a_{n-1}`).
design_choices:
**The walk is encoded as an `inductive Type` indexed by both endpoints, mirroring mathlib's `SimpleGraph.Walk`.** A flat record bundling a `List (Node × Node)` of edges with a separate coherence proof was rejected because downstream chapters (ancestor sets, acyclicity, topological order, active paths) all pattern-match on walks at every recursion step; a list-plus-witness pair would force each consumer to destructure the coherence proof one edge at a time. An un-indexed `Walk G` with endpoints in separate fields was also rejected since it would force $\ne$-equalities through every "walks from $u$ to $v$" quantifier. The middle vertex on `cons` is made explicit (departing from mathlib here) so downstream pattern matches reach it without `(v := …)` named-pattern syntax.

**The five derived walk-flavours are `Prop`-predicates on the single `Walk` type, not separate inductives.** Treating each as its own `inductive DirectedWalk` would duplicate the structural recursion and force a forgetful map plus lift-up lemmas in every downstream row; a `Subtype` was likewise dispreferred since it changes walk equality. A bundled `Bifurcation` `structure` was rejected on similar grounds — the source phrases these as yes/no classifications, and a `(p, hp)` pair carries exactly that information.

**The single-edge constraint is factored into a named helper `WalkStep` rather than inlined in `cons`.** Inlining the two-disjunct `Or` directly into the constructor would double the case count of every downstream recursion that destructures a `cons`-cell. A fourth backward-`L` disjunct is omitted because `hL_symm` collapses it into the forward `L`-case.

**The four end-node classifiers are four independent predicates, not a partition-style enum or a `Bool × Bool` flag.** They are deliberately not jointly exhaustive: the trivial walk satisfies none of them, and a bidirected first edge yields `intoStart ∧ ¬outOfStart`. An enum would need a fifth `none` constructor; a bit-pair would compress two distinct symbols ($\hus$ vs $\tuh$) into bit positions and lose the macro-grep correspondence. Each classifier reuses `G.into` / `G.outOf` from `EdgeRelations.lean` instead of re-spelling the underlying disjunctions.

**Two auxiliary predicates `IsColliderRest` and `IsBifurcationWithSplit` encode the positional case-splits.** An inline single recursion would need boolean flags or length comparisons per `cons`-cell to distinguish first / interior / last edge. Quantifying over an integer split index $k$ and indexing into `p.edges` was also rejected because it would force a $k \le \text{length}\,p$ bound to be threaded everywhere; the helper's $\mathbb{N}$-counter is decremented by structural recursion and the `nil` branch returns `False`, eliminating bound plumbing.

**A separate `IsBifurcationDirectedHingeWithSplit` helper, rather than a `Bool` flag on the general bifurcation predicate.** The two predicates differ on the $n = 1$ case — the general form admits the bidirected hinge, the directed-hinge variant excludes it per the operator addition. A shared predicate with a flag would conflate these. `IsBifurcationSource` is then a `Prop`-predicate on a candidate vertex rather than an `Option Node` accessor, avoiding sentinel values in consumers; the source position is read via `p.vertices[i + 1]?` instead of `List.get` with a threaded in-bounds proof.

**The vertex sequence is a `List Node`, not a `Finset` nor a length-indexed `Fin (n+1) → Node`.** A `Finset` is wrong on both counts (the tuple is ordered and may repeat); the indexed function would force `Fin`-arithmetic plumbing at every use site, whereas `List` unlocks mathlib's `Nodup`, `head?`, `getLast?`, `tail`, and `dropLast` API directly — and makes `IsPath` a one-liner over `List.Nodup`.

**Trivial walks satisfy the walk-class predicates vacuously (`True`), but the four end-node classifiers vacuously `False`.** The asymmetry is intentional: directed / bidirected / collider / path explicitly admit the trivial walk, but the classifiers are existentially loaded on $a_0$ or $a_{n-1}$ existing, so `False` is the faithful encoding.

**The $n = 1$ collider case is the *strict* bidirected reading, overriding the source's inline remark.** The relaxed "any adjacency" reading was rejected per the operator addition: the symbolic pattern collapses for $n = 1$ to require arrowheads at both endpoints, forcing the lone edge into `L`. Purely directed edges in either direction are excluded.
